lot: Lot RL — Canonical Tool Error Model and Rate-Limit Awareness

priority_mapping.0: P0 / This task should not be done in parallel with another.

assumptions: The MACC coordinator already has a working error-code taxonomy (E100-E900) and auto-retry framework in engine.rs.
The task_selector already supports tool_priority, max_parallel_per_tool, and tool_specializations for routing.
The TUI CoordinatorLive screen, AppState, and pause overlay exist and display active tasks, events, and errors.
The Web API exposes ApiCoordinatorStatus with pause context via /api/v1/status.
TaskRuntime already has slo_warnings (BTreeMap<String, SloWarningRecord>) and upsert_slo_warning() method.
Performer.sh and tool runners emit structured events via IPC (heartbeat, phase_result, failed).
The coordinator pause mechanism (pause file + blocking loop + TUI overlay) is fully operational.
MACC does not call provider APIs directly — it shells out to CLI tools (claude, codex, gemini). Error signals come from stderr/stdout text and exit codes, not HTTP response objects.
Each adapter already has a performer shell script (adapters/claude/claude.performer.sh, adapters/codex/codex.performer.sh, adapters/gemini/gemini.performer.sh).
The adapters crate has per-tool Rust modules (adapters/claude/src/, adapters/codex/src/, adapters/gemini/src/) and a shared crate (adapters/shared/src/).
MaccError in core/src/lib.rs currently has 8 variants; many errors are shoehorned into Validation(String).
All tasks aim to be small enough to complete in < 1 day by a single AI agent.
architecture_notes:
This lot introduces a three-layer error model:
  Layer 1 — Transport/SDK: timeout, DNS, TLS, connection errors. Detected from tool CLI stderr.
  Layer 2 — Provider API: HTTP status + provider-specific codes + messages. The same HTTP code does NOT mean the same thing per provider (e.g. Claude 429 = rate-limit, 529 = overload; OpenAI 429 = rate-limit OR quota; Gemini 429 = RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED which can be quota, overload, or functional limit). Each adapter normalizes its own errors to a canonical model.
  Layer 3 — Local tool/integration: invalid manifest, missing config, tool not found, session conflict.

Key principle: the MACC core NEVER reasons on raw provider errors. Per-adapter error normalizers (Rust, in adapters/<tool>/src/) map stderr/stdout patterns to a canonical CanonicalClass enum. The coordinator and all consumers only see normalized ToolError values.

MaccError (core/src/lib.rs) is the library error type for core API callers. ToolError/CanonicalClass (runtime error classification during performer execution) is a separate domain type in the coordinator layer. These must not be conflated.

tasks.title: Define rate-limit error codes, types, and config fields
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: MACC has no dedicated error category for rate-limit / quota exhaustion. The existing error taxonomy (E100-E900) does not distinguish transient throttling from permanent failures, and the coordinator has no types to represent tool-level rate-limit state.
Key actions:
  1. Add E600 error code series: E601 (rate-limited / 429), E602 (quota exhausted / hard limit).
  2. Define RateLimitInfo struct (tool_id, error_code, retry_after_seconds, detected_at, source_header).
  3. Define ToolThrottleState struct (tool_id, throttled_until, consecutive_429_count, backoff_seconds, last_rate_limit_info).
  4. Add coordinator config fields: rate_limit_backoff_base_seconds, rate_limit_backoff_max_seconds, rate_limit_fallback_enabled, rate_limit_throttle_parallel.
  5. Add corresponding fields to CoordinatorEnvConfig for CLI/env override.
Out of scope: Actual detection logic, backoff engine, routing changes, TUI display.
Success criteria: cargo check -p macc-core passes. All new types are Serialize+Deserialize+Clone+Debug+Default. Config fields are documented with serde defaults. Unit tests validate serialization roundtrip for all new types.
tasks.objective: Establish the contracts and vocabulary for rate-limit handling before any implementation begins.
tasks.result: New file core/src/coordinator/rate_limit.rs with RateLimitInfo, ToolThrottleState, and helper functions. E601/E602 documented alongside existing codes. CoordinatorConfig and CoordinatorEnvConfig extended with 4 new fields each. Unit tests pass.
steps: Create core/src/coordinator/rate_limit.rs with RateLimitInfo and ToolThrottleState structs.
Add E600 series constants (E601, E602) to the module with doc comments explaining the distinction (transient vs hard quota).
Add pub mod rate_limit to core/src/coordinator.rs.

Add rate_limit_backoff_base_seconds (default 30), rate_limit_backoff_max_seconds (default 300), rate_limit_fallback_enabled (default true), rate_limit_throttle_parallel (default true) to CoordinatorConfig in core/src/config/mod.rs with #[serde(default)].
Mirror the same 4 fields as Option<T> in CoordinatorEnvConfig in core/src/coordinator/types.rs.
Add resolve helper functions: resolve_rate_limit_backoff_base(), resolve_rate_limit_backoff_max(), etc. following the existing resolve_* pattern in control_plane.rs.
Write unit tests: serialization roundtrip for RateLimitInfo and ToolThrottleState, config default values.
Verify cargo check -p macc-core and cargo test -p macc-core pass.
tasks.notes: This is the contracts-first task. All subsequent tasks depend on these types. Follow the existing resolve pattern: env_cfg -> coordinator_cfg -> default. Status: code exists uncommitted in worker-02 worktree — needs commit and merge.

tasks.title: Define CanonicalClass enum and ToolError struct for provider-agnostic error normalization
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: MACC has no canonical, provider-agnostic error model. The same HTTP status means different things per provider (Claude 429 = rate-limit, 529 = overload; OpenAI 429 = rate-limit OR quota; Gemini 429 = RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED covering quota, overload, or functional limits). Without a canonical model, core logic would need if-chains on raw provider strings — fragile and unmaintainable.
Key actions:
  1. Create core/src/coordinator/error_normalizer.rs as the canonical error model module.
  2. Define CanonicalClass enum with variants: Auth, Billing, RateLimit, QuotaExhausted, Overloaded, Timeout, SessionConflict, ToolNotFound, OutputMalformed, Network, PolicyViolation, Internal, Unknown.
  3. Define ToolError struct with fields: provider (String), canonical_class (CanonicalClass), retryable (bool), retry_after_seconds (Option<u64>), user_action_required (bool), raw_message (String, first 500 chars), error_code (String, MACC E-series), request_id (Option<String>), attempt (u32), operation (String).
  4. Define ErrorNormalizer trait with fn normalize(exit_code: i32, stderr: &str, stdout: &str) -> Option<ToolError>.
  5. Map CanonicalClass to MACC E-series codes: Auth/Billing/PolicyViolation -> no retry; RateLimit -> E601; QuotaExhausted -> E602; Overloaded -> E601 (retryable); Timeout/Network -> E101 (existing); SessionConflict -> new E603; ToolNotFound -> E102; OutputMalformed -> E103; Internal -> E901.
  6. Add E603 (session conflict) to the error code taxonomy.
Out of scope: Per-adapter pattern implementations (RL-NORM-002C/D/E), integration into engine.rs.
Success criteria: cargo check -p macc-core passes. CanonicalClass and ToolError are Serialize+Deserialize+Clone+Debug. ErrorNormalizer trait is public. Unit tests validate CanonicalClass->E-series mapping and ToolError serialization roundtrip.
tasks.objective: Establish the provider-agnostic canonical error contract that all adapter normalizers will implement and all coordinator consumers will use.
tasks.result: New file core/src/coordinator/error_normalizer.rs with CanonicalClass, ToolError, ErrorNormalizer trait, canonical_to_error_code() mapping. E603 documented. Unit tests pass.
steps: Create core/src/coordinator/error_normalizer.rs.
Define CanonicalClass enum (13 variants) with Serialize, Deserialize, Clone, Debug, PartialEq, Default (default=Unknown).
Define ToolError struct with all fields, Serialize+Deserialize+Clone+Debug+Default.
Define ErrorNormalizer trait: fn normalize(&self, exit_code: i32, stderr: &str, stdout: &str) -> Option<ToolError>.
Implement canonical_to_error_code(class: &CanonicalClass) -> &'static str that maps each class to its E-series code.
Implement is_retryable(class: &CanonicalClass) -> bool that returns the default retry policy per class.
Implement is_user_action_required(class: &CanonicalClass) -> bool for classes needing human intervention (Auth, Billing, QuotaExhausted, PolicyViolation).
Add pub mod error_normalizer to core/src/coordinator.rs.
Write unit tests: CanonicalClass->error_code mapping, retryable classification, ToolError serialization roundtrip, default values.
Verify cargo check -p macc-core and cargo test -p macc-core pass.
tasks.notes: This module defines ONLY the contract — no detection logic. Detection patterns live in per-adapter normalizers (RL-NORM-002C/D/E). The ErrorNormalizer trait is implemented by each adapter. CanonicalClass intentionally has no 'Validation' or 'NotFound' variants — those are MaccError concerns (library-level), not tool runtime errors.

tasks.title: Expand MaccError with semantic domain variants
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: MaccError in core/src/lib.rs has only 8 variants. Many errors across coordinator, storage, git, fetch, and catalog operations are shoehorned into Validation(String), losing structure and making programmatic error handling impossible.
Key actions:
  1. Add Coordinator { code: &'static str, message: String } variant for coordinator logic failures (registry corruption, invalid state transition, dispatch errors).
  2. Add Storage { backend: &'static str, message: String } variant for SQLite/JSON storage failures.
  3. Add Git { operation: String, message: String } variant for git command failures.
  4. Add Fetch { url: String, message: String } variant for remote fetching failures.
  5. Add Catalog { operation: String, message: String } variant for catalog operations.
  6. Audit existing Validation(String) uses in coordinator, storage, git, and fetch code paths. Migrate the most impactful ones (>= 5 call sites per domain) to the new variants.
Out of scope: Migrating ALL Validation uses (too large). ToolError/CanonicalClass (separate domain type, not MaccError). Changing the public Result<T> alias.
Success criteria: cargo check passes for all crates. No existing tests break. At least 5 call sites per new variant are migrated from Validation. New variants have proper #[error("...")] formatting.
tasks.objective: Give MaccError enough semantic structure for downstream consumers (CLI, TUI, Web) to handle errors programmatically rather than string-matching on Validation messages.
tasks.result: MaccError gains 5 new variants. 25+ Validation(String) call sites migrated. All existing tests pass. cargo check passes for all workspace crates.
steps: In core/src/lib.rs, add the 5 new MaccError variants with appropriate #[error("...")] formatting.
Search core/src/ for MaccError::Validation uses in coordinator-related code. Migrate to Coordinator { code, message }.
Search core/src/ for Validation uses in storage/SQLite code. Migrate to Storage { backend, message }.
Search core/src/ for Validation uses in git:: module. Migrate to Git { operation, message }.
Search core/src/ for Validation uses in fetch/catalog code. Migrate to Fetch/Catalog variants.
Update any match arms in cli/src/ and tui/src/ that pattern-match on MaccError to handle the new variants (or add _ catch-all where appropriate).
Verify cargo check passes for all workspace crates (core, cli, tui, adapters).
Verify cargo test -p macc-core passes.
Verify cargo test -p macc-cli --lib passes.
tasks.notes: This is a careful refactoring task. Do NOT change error behavior — only change the variant used. Keep Validation(String) as the catch-all for truly generic validation errors. The goal is progressive enrichment, not a complete rewrite. MaccError is the library error type for core API callers. ToolError/CanonicalClass is a SEPARATE type for runtime tool error classification — do NOT conflate them.

tasks.title: Implement Claude error normalizer with pattern-matching on stderr/stdout
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: Claude's CLI (claude) emits a variety of error patterns to stderr/stdout that MACC currently treats as generic E101. The same errors carry different semantics:
  - 'API Error: 529 {...overloaded_error...}' = provider overloaded (transient, retryable)
  - 'You've hit your limit · resets Xpm' = usage cap / quota exhausted (NOT retryable, user action required)
  - 'Error: Session ID ... is already in use' = session conflict (retryable with new session)
  - '429 Too Many Requests' or 'rate_limit_error' = transient rate-limit (retryable with backoff)
  - 'authentication_error' = invalid API key (not retryable, user action)
  - 'permission_error' = not allowed (not retryable)
  - 'overloaded_error' (without 529) = same as 529
  - Claude can also fail during SSE stream after HTTP 200, so exit code 0 with error text in stdout is possible.
  - Claude includes request_id (e.g. req_xxx) in error payloads — must be extracted.
Key actions:
  1. Create adapters/shared/src/error_normalizer.rs re-exporting ErrorNormalizer trait from core for convenience.
  2. Create adapters/claude/src/error_normalizer.rs implementing ErrorNormalizer for Claude.
  3. Pattern-match on stderr+stdout using compiled regex (OnceLock<Vec<Regex>>).
  4. Extract request_id from 'request_id' JSON field if present.
  5. Extract retry-after hints from text if present.
  6. Map patterns to CanonicalClass.
Out of scope: Codex and Gemini normalizers (separate tasks). Integration into coordinator engine (RL-DETECT-002D).
Success criteria: ClaudeErrorNormalizer implements ErrorNormalizer. Unit tests cover all 8+ known Claude error patterns. request_id extraction works. cargo check -p macc-adapter-claude passes.
tasks.objective: Teach MACC's Claude adapter to classify Claude-specific error patterns into the canonical error model.
tasks.result: New file adapters/claude/src/error_normalizer.rs with ClaudeErrorNormalizer. Tests for 529, 429, quota, session conflict, auth, permission, overloaded, stream error patterns.
steps: Create adapters/shared/src/error_normalizer.rs that re-exports macc_core::coordinator::error_normalizer::{ErrorNormalizer, CanonicalClass, ToolError} for adapter convenience.
Add pub mod error_normalizer to adapters/shared/src/lib.rs.
Create adapters/claude/src/error_normalizer.rs with pub struct ClaudeErrorNormalizer.

Define compiled regex patterns using std::sync::OnceLock for: 529/overloaded_error, 429/rate_limit_error, 'hit your limit'/'usage limit', 'Session ID.*already in use', 'authentication_error', 'permission_error', 'invalid_api_key', 'billing'/'payment', request_id extraction (req_[a-zA-Z0-9]+).

Implement ErrorNormalizer::normalize() for ClaudeErrorNormalizer: combine stderr+stdout, run patterns in priority order, return first match as ToolError with correct CanonicalClass, retryable, user_action_required.
For 'hit your limit' pattern: parse reset time if present (e.g. 'resets 8pm') and compute retry_after_seconds from current time.
For request_id: extract from JSON payload field or from raw text regex.
Add pub mod error_normalizer to adapters/claude/src/lib.rs.

Write unit tests with real error strings from production logs: '529 overloaded', 'You've hit your limit · resets 8pm (UTC)', 'Session ID b2784509-... is already in use', '429 Too Many Requests', 'authentication_error', 'permission_error'.
Test that exit code 0 + error text in stdout still produces a ToolError (SSE stream failure case).
Verify cargo check -p macc-adapter-claude and cargo test -p macc-adapter-claude pass.
tasks.notes: Pattern priority matters: check quota exhaustion ('hit your limit') BEFORE generic rate-limit ('429') because quota is non-retryable while 429 is retryable. The real error strings from the coordinator logs (Run 1 and Run 2) should be used as test fixtures. Claude Code docs reference: api_error events with model, status_code, attempt, duration_ms.

tasks.title: Implement Codex/OpenAI error normalizer with pattern-matching on stderr/stdout
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: OpenAI/Codex CLI emits error patterns with different semantics than Claude for the same HTTP codes:
  - 429 can mean EITHER rate-limit OR exhausted quota/budget depending on the message.
  - 503 can take the special 'Slow Down' form when traffic ramps up too fast (surge protection).
  - SDK errors include APIConnectionError, APITimeoutError, AuthenticationError, RateLimitError, InternalServerError.
  - 'insufficient_quota' or 'budget exceeded' = hard quota, not retryable.
  - 'server_error' + 500 = internal, retryable.
  - 'invalid_api_key' = auth, not retryable.
Key actions:
  1. Create adapters/codex/src/error_normalizer.rs implementing ErrorNormalizer for Codex.
  2. Pattern-match on stderr+stdout using compiled regex.
  3. Distinguish 429 rate-limit from 429 quota based on message content ('insufficient_quota', 'budget', 'plan' vs 'Rate limit').
  4. Handle 503 'Slow Down' as Overloaded (retryable with ramp-up backoff).
  5. Extract request ID if present.
Out of scope: Claude and Gemini normalizers. Coordinator integration.
Success criteria: CodexErrorNormalizer implements ErrorNormalizer. Unit tests cover 429 rate-limit, 429 quota, 503, auth, timeout, connection patterns. cargo check -p macc-adapter-codex passes.
tasks.objective: Teach MACC's Codex adapter to classify OpenAI-specific error patterns where the same HTTP code carries different semantics.
tasks.result: New file adapters/codex/src/error_normalizer.rs with CodexErrorNormalizer. Tests for 429 (rate vs quota), 503 Slow Down, auth, timeout, connection, internal patterns.
steps: Create adapters/codex/src/error_normalizer.rs with pub struct CodexErrorNormalizer.

Define compiled regex patterns using std::sync::OnceLock for: 429+rate_limit, 429+insufficient_quota, 429+budget, 503+'Slow Down', 500/server_error, AuthenticationError/invalid_api_key, APIConnectionError, APITimeoutError, RateLimitError.
Implement ErrorNormalizer::normalize() for CodexErrorNormalizer: combine stderr+stdout, run patterns in priority order.
For 429: if message contains 'insufficient_quota' or 'budget' or 'plan' -> CanonicalClass::QuotaExhausted (E602, not retryable). Otherwise -> CanonicalClass::RateLimit (E601, retryable).
For 503 'Slow Down': -> CanonicalClass::Overloaded (E601, retryable, recommend gradual ramp-up).
For APIConnectionError: -> CanonicalClass::Network. For APITimeoutError: -> CanonicalClass::Timeout.
Extract request ID from 'request_id' or 'x-request-id' if present in output.
Add pub mod error_normalizer to adapters/codex/src/lib.rs.

Write unit tests with realistic error strings: '429 Rate limit reached', '429 You exceeded your current quota', '503 Service Unavailable: Slow Down', 'AuthenticationError: invalid_api_key', 'APIConnectionError: Connection refused', 'APITimeoutError'.
Verify cargo check -p macc-adapter-codex and cargo test -p macc-adapter-codex pass.
tasks.notes: OpenAI's 429 is ambiguous — the message body is the discriminant, not the status code. This is the core reason MACC cannot use a single if status==429 check. OpenAI also has websocket-specific errors (previous_response_not_found, websocket_connection_limit_reached) that should map to SessionConflict if encountered.

tasks.title: Implement Gemini/Vertex AI error normalizer with pattern-matching on stderr/stdout
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: Gemini via Vertex AI combines HTTP status with Google canonical error codes (RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED, UNAVAILABLE, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, etc.) that carry distinct semantics:
  - 429 + RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED can mean quota, shared overload, OR functional limits.
  - 503 + UNAVAILABLE = service temporarily down (retryable).
  - 504 + DEADLINE_EXCEEDED can result from client deadline too short (client-fixable) vs server timeout.
  - 400 + INVALID_ARGUMENT = validation, not retryable.
  - 403 + PERMISSION_DENIED = auth/permission, not retryable.
  - 404 + NOT_FOUND = model/endpoint not found.
  - Google recommends truncated exponential backoff with jitter for retriable errors.
Key actions:
  1. Create adapters/gemini/src/error_normalizer.rs implementing ErrorNormalizer for Gemini.
  2. Pattern-match on both HTTP status AND Google canonical error code in stderr/stdout.
  3. For 429 + RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: check message for 'quota' vs generic rate-limit. Quota -> E602, generic -> E601.
  4. Handle UNAVAILABLE, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, INTERNAL as retryable.
  5. Handle PERMISSION_DENIED, INVALID_ARGUMENT, NOT_FOUND as non-retryable.
Out of scope: Claude and Codex normalizers. Coordinator integration.
Success criteria: GeminiErrorNormalizer implements ErrorNormalizer. Unit tests cover RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED (quota vs rate), UNAVAILABLE, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, PERMISSION_DENIED, INVALID_ARGUMENT patterns. cargo check -p macc-adapter-gemini passes.
tasks.objective: Teach MACC's Gemini adapter to classify Vertex AI error codes where Google canonical codes add a semantic layer on top of HTTP status.
tasks.result: New file adapters/gemini/src/error_normalizer.rs with GeminiErrorNormalizer. Tests for all Google canonical error code patterns.
steps: Create adapters/gemini/src/error_normalizer.rs with pub struct GeminiErrorNormalizer.

Define compiled regex patterns using std::sync::OnceLock for: RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED, UNAVAILABLE, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, INTERNAL, PERMISSION_DENIED, INVALID_ARGUMENT, NOT_FOUND, UNAUTHENTICATED, and HTTP status codes (429, 503, 504, 400, 403, 404, 500).
Implement ErrorNormalizer::normalize() for GeminiErrorNormalizer: combine stderr+stdout, match Google canonical code first (higher priority), then HTTP status as fallback.
For RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: if message contains 'quota' or 'limit per' -> QuotaExhausted (E602). Otherwise -> RateLimit (E601).
For UNAVAILABLE: -> Overloaded (retryable). For DEADLINE_EXCEEDED: -> Timeout (retryable). For INTERNAL: -> Internal (retryable with caution).
For PERMISSION_DENIED / UNAUTHENTICATED: -> Auth (not retryable). For INVALID_ARGUMENT: -> OutputMalformed (not retryable). For NOT_FOUND: -> ToolNotFound (not retryable).
Add pub mod error_normalizer to adapters/gemini/src/lib.rs.

Write unit tests with realistic error strings: '429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: Quota exceeded', '429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: Rate limit', '503 UNAVAILABLE', '504 DEADLINE_EXCEEDED', '403 PERMISSION_DENIED', '400 INVALID_ARGUMENT'.
Verify cargo check -p macc-adapter-gemini and cargo test -p macc-adapter-gemini pass.
tasks.notes: Gemini's error model is the most structured (Google canonical codes + HTTP), which makes normalization more predictable but requires matching the canonical code text, not just the HTTP status. Vertex AI docs explicitly recommend truncated exponential backoff with jitter — our compute_backoff_delay() already implements this.

tasks.title: Integrate error normalizers into coordinator engine for canonical error classification
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: The coordinator engine (engine.rs) currently assigns E101 to all performer failures. With per-adapter ErrorNormalizers now available, the engine must route performer output through the correct normalizer to get canonical ToolError classification.
Key actions:
  1. Create a normalizer registry that maps tool_id -> ErrorNormalizer implementation.
  2. In engine.rs apply_job_completion_in_registry(), when a job fails: identify the tool, get its normalizer, run normalize(exit_code, stderr, stdout).
  3. If normalizer returns Some(ToolError), use its error_code instead of generic E101.
  4. Store the full ToolError in task_runtime.extra as serialized JSON for diagnostics.
  5. Emit a tool_error_classified event with the canonical class.
  6. Populate RateLimitInfo from ToolError when canonical_class is RateLimit or QuotaExhausted.
  7. Handle the exit-code-override case: if exit_code != 0 but stdout contains a MACC_TASK_RESULT success marker, check normalizer for transient errors that were recovered from. If recovery confirmed, treat as success.
Out of scope: Backoff delay logic, tool routing, TUI display.
Success criteria: When a performer exits with Claude 529 in stderr, the task gets CanonicalClass::Overloaded (E601) not E101. When OpenAI outputs 'insufficient_quota', the task gets E602 not E101. Event is emitted. Unit tests cover normalizer routing for all 3 adapters.
tasks.objective: Wire the per-adapter normalizers into the coordinator's error handling pipeline, replacing the current undifferentiated E101 classification.
tasks.result: Normalizer registry in engine.rs. apply_job_completion_in_registry() uses adapter-specific normalization. ToolError stored in task_runtime.extra. tool_error_classified event added to COORDINATOR_EVENT_TYPES_V1. Tests pass.
steps: Create a normalizer registry function: get_normalizer_for_tool(tool_id: &str) -> Option<Box<dyn ErrorNormalizer>> that returns ClaudeErrorNormalizer, CodexErrorNormalizer, or GeminiErrorNormalizer based on tool_id.
Add tool_error_classified to COORDINATOR_EVENT_TYPES_V1 in core/src/coordinator.rs.
In engine.rs apply_job_completion_in_registry(), before the current generic error-code assignment: get the normalizer for the task's tool_id, call normalize(exit_code, stderr, stdout).

If normalize() returns Some(ToolError): set last_error_code from tool_error.error_code, set last_error_message from tool_error.raw_message, store full ToolError as JSON in task_runtime.extra['tool_error'].
If ToolError.canonical_class is RateLimit or QuotaExhausted: build RateLimitInfo from ToolError fields and store in task_runtime.extra['rate_limit_info'].
Emit tool_error_classified event with canonical_class, error_code, retryable, request_id in payload.

Handle exit-code-override: if exit_code != 0 AND stdout contains 'MACC_TASK_RESULT: success' or 'already_satisfied', AND normalizer detected only transient errors (Overloaded, RateLimit), treat as success_with_recovery.
If normalizer returns None: fall through to the existing E101 assignment (backward compatible).
Write unit tests: mock normalizers, verify routing by tool_id, verify ToolError storage in extra, verify event emission, verify exit-code-override logic.
Verify cargo test -p macc-core passes.
tasks.notes: The normalizer registry does NOT need a trait object registry pattern — a simple match on tool_id is fine for 3 tools. If more adapters are added later, this can be refactored. The exit-code-override fix addresses the Run 2 bug where Claude returned already_satisfied but exit code was 1 due to earlier 529.

tasks.title: Implement exponential backoff engine for rate-limited tasks
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: When a task fails with E601/E602, immediately retrying will worsen the rate-limit situation. The coordinator needs a backoff engine that computes an appropriate delay before re-queuing.
Key actions:
  1. Implement compute_backoff_delay() that returns the delay in seconds using: min(base * 2^attempt, max) with jitter.
  2. If ToolError/RateLimitInfo contains retry_after_seconds, use that value instead of computed backoff.
  3. Store ToolThrottleState per tool in the coordinator runtime state.
  4. When a task fails with E601, update the tool's ToolThrottleState (increment consecutive count, set throttled_until).
  5. When a task fails with E602, set throttled_until to a longer period (or indefinite until manual resume).
  6. The existing auto-retry loop in engine.rs should respect throttled_until: if the tool is throttled, do not requeue immediately — set a delayed_until timestamp on the task.
  7. Add delayed_until field to TaskRuntime.
Out of scope: Dynamic routing (fallback to other tools), max_parallel adjustment, TUI.
Success criteria: After an E601 failure, the task is requeued with a computed delay. Consecutive 429s produce exponentially increasing delays (30s, 60s, 120s, 240s, capped at max). Retry-After header value overrides computed delay. E602 pauses the tool indefinitely. Tests validate delay computation and state tracking.
tasks.objective: Prevent retry storms by introducing intelligent delay between rate-limited retries.
tasks.result: core/src/coordinator/rate_limit.rs gains compute_backoff_delay(), update_throttle_state(). TaskRuntime gains delayed_until field. engine.rs respects delays. 6+ unit tests.
steps: Add compute_backoff_delay(attempt: usize, base_seconds: u64, max_seconds: u64, retry_after: Option<u64>) -> u64 to rate_limit.rs. Use min(base * 2^attempt, max) with 10% random jitter. If retry_after is Some, return max(retry_after, computed).

Add update_throttle_state(state: &mut ToolThrottleState, info: &RateLimitInfo, backoff: u64, now: &str) to rate_limit.rs. Increment consecutive_429_count, set throttled_until = now + backoff, store last info.
Add clear_throttle_state(state: &mut ToolThrottleState) to reset when a tool succeeds (consecutive count = 0).
Add delayed_until: Option<String> (ISO 8601) to TaskRuntime in core/src/coordinator/model.rs.
In engine.rs apply_job_completion_in_registry(), when error code is E601: compute backoff, set task.task_runtime.delayed_until, requeue to todo with reason 'rate_limit_backoff'.
In engine.rs, when error code is E602: do NOT auto-retry. Instead, transition to Blocked state with reason 'quota_exhausted'. Emit a quota_exhausted event.
Add quota_exhausted to COORDINATOR_EVENT_TYPES_V1.
Write unit tests: backoff computation (1st=30s, 2nd=60s, 3rd=120s, 4th=240s, 5th=300s cap), Retry-After override, throttle state update/clear, E602 blocks task.
Verify cargo test -p macc-core passes.
tasks.notes: Jitter should use rand or a simple deterministic hash to avoid all tasks retrying at the exact same time. If rand is not available, use a hash of task_id + attempt as pseudo-jitter.

tasks.title: Respect delayed_until during task dispatch selection
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: After RL-BACKOFF-003 sets delayed_until on rate-limited tasks, the task selector still sees them as 'todo' and tries to dispatch them immediately.
Key actions:
  1. In task_selector.rs select_next_ready_task_typed(), filter out tasks where delayed_until > now.
  2. Add is_task_delayed(task, now) helper to rate_limit.rs.
Out of scope: Tool-level throttle state checks (covered in RL-ROUTE-005).
Success criteria: A task with delayed_until in the future is not selected for dispatch. Once delayed_until passes, the task becomes eligible again. Unit tests validate both scenarios.
tasks.objective: Ensure the dispatch loop respects backoff delays and does not re-dispatch throttled tasks prematurely.
tasks.result: task_selector.rs calls is_task_delayed() during candidate filtering. 3+ unit tests.
steps: Add is_task_delayed(task: &Task, now: &str) -> bool to core/src/coordinator/rate_limit.rs. Compares delayed_until against now using ISO 8601 string comparison.
In task_selector.rs select_next_ready_task_typed(), add a filter after the state == 'todo' check: skip tasks where is_task_delayed(task, now) returns true.
Pass current timestamp (now parameter) through the selection function. Extend TaskSelectorConfig or add a now field.
Write unit tests: task with future delayed_until is skipped, task with past delayed_until is eligible, task without delayed_until is eligible.
Verify cargo test -p macc-core passes.
tasks.notes: ISO 8601 string comparison works for chronological ordering (YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ). No chrono dependency needed for the comparison itself.

tasks.title: Implement dynamic tool fallback routing on rate-limit
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: When the primary tool (e.g. Claude) is rate-limited, the coordinator blocks rather than trying the next tool in tool_priority. The existing pick_tool() in task_selector.rs checks per-tool capacity but not rate-limit state.
Key actions:
  1. Maintain a ToolThrottleRegistry (BTreeMap<String, ToolThrottleState>) in the coordinator runtime.
  2. Extend pick_tool() to skip tools that are currently throttled (throttled_until > now).
  3. When the primary tool is throttled and rate_limit_fallback_enabled is true, select the next available tool from tool_priority.
  4. When ALL tools are throttled, return None (no dispatch possible until earliest throttle expires).
  5. On successful task completion, clear the throttle state for that tool.
  6. Emit a tool_fallback event when a task is assigned to a non-primary tool due to rate-limiting.
  7. Only allow automatic fallback for idempotent operations (dev phase). Review/fix phases should not be rerouted to a different tool mid-task.
Out of scope: Concurrency throttle (RL-THROTTLE-006), TUI display.
Success criteria: When tool A is throttled, tasks are dispatched to tool B (next in priority). When all tools are throttled, dispatch pauses. When tool A recovers, it resumes as primary. Unit tests cover fallback chain, all-throttled, recovery, and idempotency guard scenarios.
tasks.objective: Keep the project moving forward by routing tasks to non-throttled tools instead of blocking on a single provider's quota.
tasks.result: ToolThrottleRegistry type added to rate_limit.rs. pick_tool() checks throttle state. tool_fallback event emitted. 6+ unit tests.
steps: Add ToolThrottleRegistry type alias (BTreeMap<String, ToolThrottleState>) to rate_limit.rs.
Add is_tool_throttled(registry: &ToolThrottleRegistry, tool_id: &str, now: &str) -> bool helper.
Add next_throttle_expiry(registry: &ToolThrottleRegistry) -> Option<String> helper (returns earliest throttled_until across all tools).
Extend TaskSelectorConfig with throttle_registry: Option<ToolThrottleRegistry> field.
In task_selector.rs pick_tool(), after building the candidate list, filter out tools where is_tool_throttled() returns true (only when rate_limit_fallback_enabled is true in config).
Add idempotency guard: only allow fallback for dev phase tasks. If task.current_phase is review or fix, do not reroute.
In control_plane.rs dispatch_ready_tasks_native(), pass the current ToolThrottleRegistry into TaskSelectorConfig.
On successful job completion in engine.rs, call clear_throttle_state() for the task's tool.
Add tool_fallback to COORDINATOR_EVENT_TYPES_V1. Emit when a task is assigned to a non-primary tool.
Write unit tests: primary throttled -> fallback to secondary, all throttled -> None returned, throttle cleared on success, fallback disabled -> no routing change, review phase -> no fallback.
Verify cargo test -p macc-core passes.
tasks.notes: The ToolThrottleRegistry should be stored in CoordinatorRunState (runtime.rs) for the async control plane, and passed as a parameter for the sync task_selector. Do not store it in the persistent registry (it's volatile runtime state). Idempotency guard is critical — switching tools mid-review could produce inconsistent results.

tasks.title: Dynamically reduce max_parallel on rate-limit detection
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: A high max_parallel value saturates TPM (tokens-per-minute) limits quickly. When the first rate-limit is detected, the coordinator should proactively reduce concurrency to smooth out resource consumption.
Key actions:
  1. Track effective_max_parallel (mutable at runtime) in CoordinatorRunState, initialized from config max_parallel.
  2. When a rate-limit E601 is detected and rate_limit_throttle_parallel is true: reduce effective_max_parallel by 1 (min 1).
  3. When a tool recovers (successful completion after throttle): increase effective_max_parallel by 1 (up to original max_parallel).
  4. Log concurrency changes via the coordinator logger.
  5. Emit concurrency_adjusted event.
Out of scope: TUI display of dynamic concurrency (covered in RL-TUI-007).
Success criteria: First E601 reduces parallel from N to N-1. Recovery restores parallel from N-1 to N. Never drops below 1. Never exceeds original config value. Unit tests validate the ramp-down and ramp-up logic.
tasks.objective: Prevent rate-limit cascades by reducing concurrency pressure when throttling is detected.
tasks.result: CoordinatorRunState gains effective_max_parallel and original_max_parallel fields. Dispatch loop uses effective_max_parallel. concurrency_adjusted event added. 4+ unit tests.
steps: Add effective_max_parallel: usize and original_max_parallel: usize to CoordinatorRunState in runtime.rs. Initialize both from config max_parallel.
Add reduce_parallel(state: &mut CoordinatorRunState) -> usize and restore_parallel(state: &mut CoordinatorRunState) -> usize helpers in rate_limit.rs.
In control_plane.rs dispatch_ready_tasks_native(), use state.effective_max_parallel instead of the config value for the dispatch limit check.
In engine.rs, when E601 is detected and rate_limit_throttle_parallel config is true, call reduce_parallel().
In engine.rs, when a job completes successfully AND the tool was previously throttled, call restore_parallel().
Add concurrency_adjusted to COORDINATOR_EVENT_TYPES_V1. Emit with old_value and new_value in payload.
Log via coordinator logger: 'Rate-limit detected: reducing max_parallel from N to N-1' and 'Tool recovered: restoring max_parallel from N-1 to N'.
Write unit tests: reduce from 4 to 3 on first 429, reduce from 3 to 2 on second 429, never below 1, restore from 2 to 3 on success, never above original.
Verify cargo test -p macc-core passes.
tasks.notes: This is deliberately conservative — reduce by 1, not halve. The combination with backoff delays means the system self-heals gradually rather than oscillating.

tasks.title: Fix performer exit-code propagation and add structured rate-limit error emission
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: Two critical bugs in the performer shell scripts:
  Bug 1 (P0): claude.performer.sh exits with code 1 even when the tool successfully completed the task, because an earlier transient API error (e.g. 529 overloaded) set a failure exit path that was not cleared by subsequent recovery. This was observed in Run 2 where Claude printed 'MACC_TASK_RESULT: already_satisfied' but the script still exited 1.
  Bug 2 (P1): performer.sh and tool runners use ad-hoc grep patterns to detect rate limits. This detection is not standardized and does not emit structured error codes via IPC.
Key actions:
  1. Fix exit-code override in claude.performer.sh: after tool execution, parse stdout for MACC_TASK_RESULT success markers. If a success marker is found, override exit code to 0 regardless of earlier transient errors.
  2. Apply the same fix to codex.performer.sh and gemini.performer.sh.
  3. In performer.sh, after tool execution failure, run a rate-limit detection pass on stderr/stdout.
  4. If a rate-limit pattern is detected, emit a structured 'failed' event with error_code E601 or E602 via IPC.
  5. Include Retry-After value if found in the output.
  6. Standardize detection patterns across all tool runners.
Out of scope: Rust-side normalizers (RL-NORM tasks handle that independently). This task ensures shell-side also emits clean signals.
Success criteria: When claude tool prints MACC_TASK_RESULT success marker, performer exits 0 even if earlier stderr contained API errors. When a tool runner encounters a 429, performer.sh emits a JSON event with error_code=E601 via IPC. When 'quota exceeded' is detected, error_code=E602.
tasks.objective: Fix the P0 exit-code bug that caused false failures in Run 2, and ensure the shell-based performer pipeline emits clean, structured rate-limit signals.
tasks.result: claude.performer.sh (and codex/gemini) gains MACC_TASK_RESULT exit-code override. performer.sh gains detect_rate_limit() function with IPC emission. Works for all tool runners.
steps: In adapters/claude/claude.performer.sh, after run_and_capture or run_resume_and_capture: scan $output_capture for 'MACC_TASK_RESULT: success_with_changes', 'MACC_TASK_RESULT: success_without_changes', or 'MACC_TASK_RESULT: already_satisfied'. If found, set rc=0.
Apply the same exit-code override pattern to adapters/codex/codex.performer.sh and adapters/gemini/gemini.performer.sh.

Add detect_rate_limit() function to automat/performer.sh that checks combined stderr+stdout for known patterns: 429, RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED, 'rate limit', 'quota exceeded', 'Too Many Requests', 'usage limit reached', 529, 'overloaded'.
Parse Retry-After header value if present in the output.
Classify as E601 (transient: 429, rate limit, overloaded) or E602 (hard quota: 'quota exceeded', 'hit your limit', 'usage limit reached') using the same heuristics as the Rust normalizers.
After tool execution failure, call detect_rate_limit(). If matched, set LAST_ERROR_CODE to E601 or E602 (instead of generic E101) in the error payload.
Ensure the exit code from performer.sh is non-zero for actual failures (but the error_code in the event allows smart coordinator handling).
Remove ad-hoc rate-limit grep patterns from individual runner scripts and consolidate into the shared detect_rate_limit() function.
Test manually with simulated rate-limit output (echo fake error strings into a temp file and run the detection function).
tasks.notes: The exit-code override is the highest-priority fix. It addresses the exact bug observed in coordinator Run 2 (RL-TYPES-001 implementation). The Rust-side normalizers (RL-NORM tasks) are a safety net — both sides detecting the same thing is intentional redundancy. Shell detection is simpler (grep patterns) but runs first (at performer exit). Rust detection is more sophisticated (regex + context) and runs in the coordinator.

tasks.title: Display rate-limit status and quota warnings in TUI CoordinatorLive
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: When tools are rate-limited, the TUI shows 'Failed' status which is misleading. Users must dig into logs to understand the situation. The CoordinatorLive screen should show rate-limit state, throttled tools, and dynamic concurrency.
Key actions:
  1. Extend CoordinatorTaskSnapshot with throttled_tools (list of tool IDs + throttled_until).
  2. Extend AppState with coordinator_throttled_tools and coordinator_effective_max_parallel.
  3. In CoordinatorLive rendering, display a 'Throttled Tools' section below the active tasks.
  4. When a tool is throttled, show: 'tool_id: throttled until HH:MM:SS (backoff: Ns, consecutive: N)'.
  5. Show effective_max_parallel vs original: 'Concurrency: 2/4 (throttled)'.
  6. When E602 (quota exhausted) triggers a pause, display a specific banner: 'PAUSED: Quota exhausted for <tool_id>. Resume after quota resets or switch tool.' instead of generic pause message.
  7. In active tasks display, show 'rate-limited' runtime status instead of 'failed' for E601 tasks.
Out of scope: Web API changes (RL-WEB-008).
Success criteria: TUI CoordinatorLive shows throttled tools section. Rate-limited tasks show 'rate-limited' not 'failed'. Quota exhaustion pause banner is specific. Concurrency display shows effective/original.
tasks.objective: Give operators immediate visibility into rate-limit state without needing to read logs.
tasks.result: tui/src/state.rs AppState extended. tui/src/lib.rs CoordinatorLive rendering updated. Pause overlay shows quota-specific message for E602.
steps: Add ThrottledToolInfo struct (tool_id, throttled_until, consecutive_count, backoff_seconds) to tui/src/state.rs.
Add coordinator_throttled_tools: Vec<ThrottledToolInfo> and coordinator_effective_max_parallel: Option<(usize, usize)> (effective, original) to AppState.
In the CoordinatorLive refresh logic, populate throttled_tools from the coordinator status or events.
In tui/src/lib.rs CoordinatorLive render, add a 'Throttled Tools' block between the task snapshot and active tasks. Show each throttled tool with countdown.
Modify the active tasks display: when task.last_error_code starts with 'E601', show runtime_status as 'rate-limited' (amber color) instead of 'failed' (red).
Modify the concurrency display line to show 'Concurrency: N/M (throttled)' when effective < original.

In render_coordinator_pause_overlay(), check if pause_reason contains 'quota_exhausted' or error code E602. If so, show specific message: 'Quota exhausted for <tool>. Options: wait for quota reset, switch to another tool, or resume manually.'
Verify cargo check -p macc-tui passes.
tasks.notes: Colors: rate-limited = amber/yellow, quota-exhausted = red with bold. Use the existing theme system (t.warn for amber, t.bad for red).

tasks.title: Expose rate-limit status in Web API
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: The Web API (/api/v1/status) does not expose rate-limit state. External dashboards and monitoring tools cannot observe throttling.
Key actions:
  1. Extend ApiCoordinatorStatus with throttled_tools array and effective_max_parallel.
  2. Extend CoordinatorStatus in core with the same fields.
  3. Populate the fields from the runtime throttle registry when building status.
Out of scope: TUI changes (RL-TUI-007), detection logic.
Success criteria: GET /api/v1/status returns throttled_tools array (may be empty) and effective_max_parallel. When a tool is throttled, the API response includes it. Existing tests still pass.
tasks.objective: Enable external observability of rate-limit state via the REST API.
tasks.result: core/src/service/coordinator_workflow.rs CoordinatorStatus extended. cli/src/commands/web.rs ApiCoordinatorStatus extended. Tests updated.
steps: Add throttled_tools: Vec<ThrottledToolStatus> and effective_max_parallel: Option<usize> to CoordinatorStatus in coordinator_workflow.rs. ThrottledToolStatus has tool_id: String, throttled_until: String, consecutive_count: usize.
Add matching fields to ApiCoordinatorStatus in web.rs with #[serde(skip_serializing_if = ...)] for empty/None.
In get_coordinator_status(), populate throttled_tools from the ToolThrottleRegistry if available (read from runtime state or a persisted snapshot).
Update the test fixture in web.rs to include throttled_tools: vec![] and effective_max_parallel: None.
Verify cargo check -p macc-cli --tests passes.
tasks.notes: The Web API is JSON-only. Use skip_serializing_if to keep responses clean when no throttling is active.

tasks.title: Add E601/E603 to default retry list, update documentation, and add TUI settings
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: E601 (rate-limit) and E603 (session conflict) must be in the default error_code_retry_list so these errors are auto-retried. E602 must NOT be in the retry list (hard quota should block, not retry). All new types, config fields, and error codes need documentation.
Key actions:
  1. Update the default error_code_retry_list from 'E101,E102,E103,E301,E302,E303' to 'E101,E102,E103,E301,E302,E303,E601,E603'.
  2. Document the canonical error model, CanonicalClass enum, and per-adapter normalizers in MACC.md.
  3. Document all new coordinator settings in MACC.md and README.md.
  4. Update the TUI Automation Settings screen to include the 4 new rate-limit fields.
Out of scope: Implementation of backoff or routing (already covered).
Success criteria: E601 and E603 are auto-retried by default. E602 is NOT auto-retried. MACC.md documents E600 series, canonical error model, all new config fields, and the backoff/fallback behavior. TUI shows 4 new fields in Automation Settings.
tasks.objective: Ensure rate-limit handling works out of the box with safe defaults and is fully documented.
tasks.result: Default retry list updated in control_plane.rs. MACC.md §3.4.5.1 extended with E600 series. New section on canonical error model. README.md updated. TUI Automation screen renders 4 new fields.
steps: In core/src/coordinator/control_plane.rs, update the default error_code_retry_list string to include E601 and E603.
In MACC.md §3.4.5.1 (Error codes), add E600 series: E601 (rate-limited / transient 429), E602 (quota exhausted / hard limit), E603 (session conflict).
Add a new section in MACC.md describing the three-layer error model (transport, provider API, local tool) and the canonical error normalization architecture.
Document the CanonicalClass enum values and their mapping to E-series codes.
In MACC.md §12.3 (Coordinator command), document the 4 new config fields: rate_limit_backoff_base_seconds, rate_limit_backoff_max_seconds, rate_limit_fallback_enabled, rate_limit_throttle_parallel.
Add a new subsection §12.3.5 'Rate-limit handling' in MACC.md describing the full flow: detection -> normalization -> classification -> backoff -> fallback -> throttle -> visibility.
Update README.md coordinator section with brief rate-limit handling description.
In tui/src/lib.rs Automation Settings screen, add input fields for the 4 new coordinator settings following the existing pattern.
Mirror documentation changes from MACC.md to CLAUDE.md.
Verify cargo check -p macc-tui passes.
tasks.notes: The TUI Automation screen already renders 27 coordinator parameters. Adding 4 more follows the same pattern (scroll list with label + input). E603 is for session conflicts (e.g. 'Session ID already in use') — retrying with a new session is safe.

tasks.title: Integration test: full rate-limit lifecycle with canonical error normalization
tasks.description:
Problem/Goal: Individual unit tests validate each component, but the full lifecycle (normalization -> classification -> backoff -> fallback -> concurrency reduction -> recovery) needs end-to-end validation covering all three error layers.
Key actions:
  1. Write an integration test in core/tests/ that simulates a rate-limit scenario.
  2. Use a mock task registry with 3 tasks and 2 tools.
  3. Simulate Claude 529 stderr -> verify ClaudeErrorNormalizer produces CanonicalClass::Overloaded, E601.
  4. Simulate OpenAI 429 + 'insufficient_quota' -> verify CodexErrorNormalizer produces CanonicalClass::QuotaExhausted, E602.
  5. Simulate Gemini RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED -> verify GeminiErrorNormalizer produces CanonicalClass::RateLimit, E601.
  6. Verify: task requeued with delay, tool throttled, fallback dispatch uses different tool, effective_max_parallel reduced.
  7. Simulate exit-code-override: exit 1 + 'MACC_TASK_RESULT: already_satisfied' + 529 in stderr -> verify treated as success.
  8. Simulate recovery: successful completion -> throttle cleared, max_parallel restored.
Out of scope: TUI/Web display testing (visual, not automatable here).
Success criteria: Integration test passes end-to-end. All state transitions verified. All 3 adapter normalizers exercised. Exit-code override tested. Test runs in < 10 seconds.
tasks.objective: Validate the complete canonical error normalization and rate-limit handling pipeline when all components are wired together.
tasks.result: New file core/tests/rate_limit_integration.rs with 5 test scenarios covering the full lifecycle across all 3 adapters.
steps: Create core/tests/rate_limit_integration.rs.
Build a mock TaskRegistry with 3 tasks (todo state) and a TaskSelectorConfig with tool_priority: [claude, codex], max_parallel: 4.

Scenario 1 (Claude 529 -> Overloaded): Simulate stderr='API Error: 529 {"type":"error","error":{"type":"overloaded_error"...}}'. Verify ClaudeErrorNormalizer returns CanonicalClass::Overloaded, E601, retryable=true. Verify request_id extracted.

Scenario 2 (OpenAI quota -> QuotaExhausted): Simulate stderr='429 You exceeded your current quota'. Verify CodexErrorNormalizer returns CanonicalClass::QuotaExhausted, E602, retryable=false, user_action_required=true.
Scenario 3 (Gemini RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED -> RateLimit): Simulate stderr='429 RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED: Rate limit'. Verify GeminiErrorNormalizer returns CanonicalClass::RateLimit, E601, retryable=true.

Scenario 4 (exit-code override): Simulate exit_code=1, stdout contains 'MACC_TASK_RESULT: already_satisfied', stderr contains '529 overloaded_error'. Verify the task is treated as success (exit-code override logic).

Scenario 5 (backoff + throttle + recovery): Use rate_limit.rs functions: compute_backoff_delay, update_throttle_state, is_tool_throttled, clear_throttle_state. Verify E601 -> task delayed, tool throttled -> fallback, success -> throttle cleared, parallel restored.
Ensure all assertions use the public API from error_normalizer, rate_limit, and task_selector modules.
Verify cargo test -p macc-core -- rate_limit_integration passes.
tasks.notes: This test does not require git or filesystem — it operates on in-memory types. Use real error strings from production logs as test fixtures. The test should exercise all 3 adapter normalizers to ensure they classify the same HTTP status differently based on provider context.
